title: Eva as religion Twilight thread tangent
display_title: Eva as religion Twilight thread tangent
wikitext:
[]: Post by J_Faulkner » Fri Dec 18, 2009 4:35 pm Hexon.Arq wrote: I'm impressed by anyone who was able to sit through the first movie. Perhaps there ought to be a religion.

[]: Post by Merridian » Fri Dec 18, 2009 5:03 pm J_Faulkner wrote: 1. A sizeable portion of the fanbase have mental issues, since one of the reasons for Evangelion's popularity and alleged incredible, awe-inspiring depth is the ability of these fans to tune in with the mental issues of the characters. This means that these fans have a strong propensity for emphasizing with each other's personas.

[]: Post by Eva Yojimbo » Fri Dec 18, 2009 5:47 pm ^ Merridian pretty much covered all of my thoughts about JF's post and since there's a kind of "ban" against us directly discussing things I won't add anything else.

[]: Post by Xard » Fri Dec 18, 2009 5:49 pm Mugwump: I just listened to first two ones. You rock man! Eva Yojimbo wrote: ^ Merridian pretty much covered all of my thoughts about JF's post and since there's a kind of "ban" against us directly discussing things I won't add anything else.

[]: Post by Mr. Tines » Fri Dec 18, 2009 5:54 pm J_Faulkner wrote: I think Evangelion lends itself especially well to being a religion.

[]: Post by J_Faulkner » Fri Dec 18, 2009 6:18 pm Merridian wrote: Besides, assuming that an audience enjoys a work due solely because of their ability to “relate” to the character(s) isn’t a very good assumption; I can’t stand Shinji Ikari’s attitude for most of the show, but there’s so much more that NGE offers I can look past that.

[]: Post by Mr. Tines » Fri Dec 18, 2009 6:36 pm TOPIC, bitches. Let's see those vampires sparkle!

[]: Post by Eva Yojimbo » Fri Dec 18, 2009 7:32 pm @Tines : Request Split? Maybe title it "Fans/Fandoms" or something along those lines. Xard wrote: ....wat

[]: Post by Merridian » Fri Dec 18, 2009 8:49 pm J_Faulkner wrote: they can relate to the characters in some emotional, perhaps even personal way. Now this isn't something which is always possible for other anime, obvious examples being trashy harem anime like Tenchi is (a) useless (person).

[]: Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sat Dec 19, 2009 3:53 am Merridian wrote: It all depends on whether the “discussion” is rooted in evidence, clarity, and logic.

[]: Post by tomrule123 » Sat Dec 19, 2009 10:17 am You know, it's the damnest thing. I am a roman catholic and though there are christian symbols in this show, I don't feel offended. Why? Because the regligion presented in this show is not trying to make fun or make a negative side about christianity. It is just used to make the show more mysterious and makes "more beef" to the story (though it has been done before, but not as much as this) since christianity is very uncommon in Japan. But in the end, that is what made the show popular

[]: Post by Merridian » Sat Dec 19, 2009 10:54 am Jimbo wrote: It seems that high-level academia is what most think of when they think of intellectualism but what about the idea of autodidacticism?

[]: Post by J_Faulkner » Sat Dec 19, 2009 3:03 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: The forum staff remembers our epic wars on Anime Nation Forums that bloated 2 or 3 threads into 50+ page monsters full of posts that ran over the word-limit.

[]: Post by chaosakita » Sat Dec 19, 2009 3:05 pm J_Faulkner wrote: I think Evangelion lends itself especially well to being a religion. The reasoning is this: 1. A sizeable portion of the fanbase have mental issues, since one of the reasons for Evangelion's popularity and alleged incredible, awe-inspiring depth is the ability of these fans to tune in with the mental issues of the characters (like a Biblical revelation perhaps?). This means that these fans have a strong propensity for emphathizing with each other's personas.

[]: Post by Merridian » Sat Dec 19, 2009 4:30 pm chaosakita wrote: I'm curious, since when did most college graduates go to graduate/professional school?

[]: Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sat Dec 19, 2009 8:35 pm @Merridian Merridian wrote: High-level academia almost always carries far more respect in public than someone who just researches things on their own time without the credentials... it is the acknowledgement from said academics that often brings respect in the field, otherwise you’re looked at as just another pseudo-intellectual regardless of how solid your knowledge base is... Otherwise he/she’s just a solipsist accumulator of knowledge without any way to use it…

[]: Post by Merridian » Sat Dec 19, 2009 11:42 pm Jimbo wrote: 7. a person who places a high value on or pursues things of interest to the intellect or the more complex forms and fields of knowledge, as aesthetic or philosophical matters, esp. on an abstract and general level.

[]: Post by Sailor Star Dust » Sun Dec 20, 2009 1:22 am Mr. Tines wrote: TOPIC, bitches. Let's see those vampires sparkle!

[]: Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sun Dec 20, 2009 2:07 am Merridian wrote: This is probably the best definition to stick with, though I was more referring to social labels... and labels rarely adhere to their literal dictionary counterparts

[]: Post by J_Faulkner » Sun Dec 20, 2009 2:04 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: Yet, especially in this modern age of the internet and the incredible proliferation of information, there are far more resources for individuals to learn instead of just in the formal, academic setting.
